answer:
Design a Playwright E2E Testing Strategy

1. Definition & Context

Designing a Playwright E2E testing strategy means establishing which user journeys to automate, how to structure test files, how to manage test data, and how to keep tests fast and reliable. A well-designed strategy follows the testing pyramid — E2E tests cover only critical user flows (login, data creation, purchase), leaving edge cases to unit and integration tests. Playwright's auto-waiting, trace recording, and multi-browser support make it the ideal choice for modern full-stack applications.

2. Real Project Use-Case — ContactsHub

In Manoj's ContactsHub (React 18, Fastify, MongoDB), the Playwright E2E strategy covered 60+ tests organized into 6 feature modules: authentication, CRUD operations, CSV bulk import, avatar upload with Canvas cropping, search/filter, and PWA offline behavior. Each module used a dedicated test fixture that seeded the database with test data via the Fastify API (not direct DB access), ensuring tests exercised the same code paths as real users. The strategy included cross-browser testing on Chromium, Firefox, and WebKit, catching a Safari-specific Canvas blob format issue that broke avatar uploads.

3. E2E Strategy Architecture

4. Code Example

// playwright.config.ts — ContactsHub configuration
import { defineConfig, devices } from '@playwright/test';

export default defineConfig({
  testDir: './e2e',
  timeout: 30_000,
  retries: process.env.CI ? 2 : 0,
  workers: process.env.CI ? 2 : 4,
  use: {
    baseURL: 'http://localhost:3000',
    trace: 'on-first-retry',
    screenshot: 'only-on-failure',
    video: 'retain-on-failure'
  },
  projects: [
    { name: 'chromium', use: { ...devices['Desktop Chrome'] } },
    { name: 'firefox', use: { ...devices['Desktop Firefox'] } },
    { name: 'webkit', use: { ...devices['Desktop Safari'] } },
    { name: 'mobile', use: { ...devices['iPhone 13'] } }
  ],
  webServer: {
    command: 'npm run dev',
    port: 3000,
    reuseExistingServer: !process.env.CI
  }
});

// e2e/fixtures/test-data.ts — Shared test fixtures
import { test as base, expect } from '@playwright/test';

type TestFixtures = {
  seededContact: { id: string; name: string; email: string };
  authToken: string;
};

export const test = base.extend<TestFixtures>({
  authToken: async ({ request }, use) => {
    const res = await request.post('/api/auth/login', {
      data: { email: 'test@hub.com', password: 'Test123!' }
    });
    const { token } = await res.json();
    await use(token);
  },
  seededContact: async ({ request, authToken }, use) => {
    const res = await request.post('/api/contacts', {
      data: { name: 'Seed Contact', email: `seed-${Date.now()}@test.com`, phone: '+91 9999999999' },
      headers: { authorization: `Bearer ${authToken}` }
    });
    const contact = await res.json();
    await use(contact);
    // Cleanup after test
    await request.delete(`/api/contacts/${contact.id}`, {
      headers: { authorization: `Bearer ${authToken}` }
    });
  }
});

// e2e/contacts/crud.spec.ts — CRUD E2E tests
import { test } from '../fixtures/test-data';
import { expect } from '@playwright/test';

test.describe('Contact CRUD', () => {
  test('creates a new contact and verifies in list', async ({ page }) => {
    await page.goto('/contacts');
    await page.click('[data-testid="add-contact-btn"]');
    await page.fill('[name="name"]', 'E2E Test User');
    await page.fill('[name="email"]', `e2e-${Date.now()}@test.com`);
    await page.fill('[name="phone"]', '+91 8888888888');
    await page.click('[type="submit"]');

    await expect(page.locator('.toast-success')).toBeVisible();
    await expect(page.locator('text=E2E Test User')).toBeVisible();
  });

  test('edits an existing contact', async ({ page, seededContact }) => {
    await page.goto(`/contacts/${seededContact.id}/edit`);
    await page.fill('[name="name"]', 'Updated Name');
    await page.click('[type="submit"]');
    await expect(page.locator('text=Updated Name')).toBeVisible();
  });

  test('deletes contact with confirmation', async ({ page, seededContact }) => {
    await page.goto('/contacts');
    await page.click(`[data-testid="delete-${seededContact.id}"]`);
    await page.click('[data-testid="confirm-delete"]');
    await expect(page.locator(`text=${seededContact.name}`)).not.toBeVisible();
  });
});

5. Debugging Narrative

In ContactsHub, Manoj's Playwright E2E test for CSV import passed on Chromium but failed on WebKit (Safari) with a timeout. The test clicked the file upload button, but the file chooser dialog never opened on WebKit. Debugging with Playwright's trace viewer showed that WebKit required the file input to be visible (not hidden with CSS display: none). The React component used a hidden <input type="file"> triggered by a button click — this pattern worked on Chromium but not WebKit. Manoj fixed it by using page.setInputFiles('[data-testid="csv-input"]', filePath) instead of simulating a click on the hidden input, which works across all browsers. This cross-browser bug would never have been caught without multi-browser E2E testing.

6. Pitfall & Mitigation

PitfallMitigation
Tests depend on database state from previous testsUse fixtures that seed data via API before each test and clean up after; never rely on test execution order
Flaky tests due to race conditions (element not yet rendered)Use Playwright's built-in auto-waiting (expect(locator).toBeVisible()) instead of waitForTimeout
E2E test suite takes 10+ minutes, blocking CIRun critical path tests on every push; run full cross-browser suite on merge to main only
Hardcoded test data causes conflicts in parallel runsGenerate unique identifiers (timestamp/UUID) in fixtures; use Date.now() in emails

7. Validation Strategy

  • Critical path coverage: Verify that signup → login → create contact → edit → delete → logout is covered as a single user journey test
  • Cross-browser gate: CI must pass on Chromium + WebKit + Firefox before merging to main
  • Performance budget: Full E2E suite must complete within 5 minutes on CI; individual tests within 30 seconds
  • Flake detection: Track test retry rates — any test retrying more than 10% of the time gets flagged for investigation

Debug Playwright E2E Test Failures

1. Definition & Context

Debugging Playwright E2E tests requires understanding the full stack — browser rendering, network requests, JavaScript execution, and backend state. Unlike unit test failures (which point to a specific function), E2E failures can stem from any layer: a CSS change hiding an element, a slow API response causing a timeout, a database state issue, or a genuine application bug. Playwright provides powerful debugging tools: trace viewer, screenshot on failure, video recording, and the --debug flag for step-by-step execution.

2. Real Project Use-Case — ContactsHub

In Manoj's ContactsHub, a Playwright test uploads avatar and displays cropped preview started failing in CI after a React dependency update. The test passed locally on macOS but failed on the Ubuntu CI runner. The failure showed a timeout on expect(page.locator('[data-testid="avatar-preview"]')).toBeVisible(). Manoj used the CI's Playwright trace artifact to investigate — the trace showed the Canvas API's toBlob() callback was never firing because the CI's Chromium build lacked JPEG encoding support (a headless-specific issue). The fix was adding --disable-gpu to Chromium launch args and falling back to PNG format in tests.

3. Debugging Workflow Diagram

4. Debugging Code Example

// ContactsHub — Debugging avatar upload E2E test
import { test, expect } from '@playwright/test';

test('uploads avatar and displays cropped preview', async ({ page }) => {
  await page.goto('/contacts/1/edit');

  // Upload image file
  const fileInput = page.locator('[data-testid="avatar-input"]');
  await fileInput.setInputFiles('e2e/fixtures/test-avatar.jpg');

  // Wait for crop modal
  await expect(page.locator('[data-testid="crop-modal"]')).toBeVisible();

  // Click "Apply Crop"
  await page.click('[data-testid="apply-crop"]');

  // DEBUGGING: This assertion timed out in CI
  // Step 1: Add screenshot before the failing assertion
  await page.screenshot({ path: 'debug-before-preview.png' });

  // Step 2: Check if the element exists in DOM (even if not visible)
  const previewExists = await page.locator('[data-testid="avatar-preview"]').count();
  console.log('Preview element count:', previewExists);

  // Step 3: Check for JavaScript errors
  page.on('pageerror', (err) => {
    console.log('Page error:', err.message);
  });

  // Step 4: Check network — was the upload API called?
  const uploadResponse = page.waitForResponse(
    (res) => res.url().includes('/api/upload') && res.status() === 200
  );

  // Step 5: Wait with explicit timeout for better error message
  await expect(page.locator('[data-testid="avatar-preview"]')).toBeVisible({
    timeout: 10_000 // 10s with clear failure message
  });
});

// FIX: Canvas toBlob fallback for headless Chromium
// In the React component:
canvas.toBlob(
  (blob) => {
    if (!blob) {
      // Fallback: use toDataURL instead of toBlob (works in headless)
      const dataUrl = canvas.toDataURL('image/png');
      const byteString = atob(dataUrl.split(',')[1]);
      const ab = new ArrayBuffer(byteString.length);
      const ia = new Uint8Array(ab);
      for (let i = 0; i < byteString.length; i++) {
        ia[i] = byteString.charCodeAt(i);
      }
      blob = new Blob([ab], { type: 'image/png' });
    }
    uploadAvatar(blob);
  },
  'image/jpeg', 0.9
);

5. Debugging Narrative

Manoj's step-by-step investigation of the ContactsHub avatar upload failure:

  1. CI artifact: Downloaded the failure screenshot — showed the crop modal was open but no preview image rendered after clicking "Apply Crop".
  2. Trace viewer: Opened npx playwright show-trace trace.zip — saw the toBlob callback in the action log returned null instead of a Blob object.
  3. Console errors: The trace's console tab showed: "Failed to encode image to JPEG" — a Chromium headless-specific limitation.
  4. Repro locally: Ran npx playwright test --headed — passed (headed Chromium has JPEG support). Ran with --headed=false — reproduced the failure.
  5. Fix: Added a null-check fallback in the React component's toBlob callback that uses toDataURL('image/png') when toBlob returns null. This fixed both the CI test and a rare production bug on low-memory mobile devices.

6. Pitfall & Mitigation

PitfallMitigation
Test passes locally but fails in CI due to headless browser differencesAlways run CI tests with trace/screenshot artifacts; test in headed and headless modes before pushing
Using waitForTimeout(3000) instead of proper waitsUse expect(locator).toBeVisible(), waitForResponse(), or waitForURL() — Playwright auto-waits
Flaky selectors break when UI changes (e.g., text=Submit)Use data-testid attributes for all interactive elements; never select by visible text or CSS class
Trace files are too large to store in CI artifactsUse trace: 'on-first-retry' — only generates traces for failing tests, not passing ones

7. Validation Strategy

  • CI artifact review: Every failed E2E test must produce a screenshot + trace file; CI pipeline publishes these as downloadable artifacts
  • Local repro script: npm run test:e2e:debug runs Playwright with --headed --debug for step-by-step investigation
  • Headless parity: Run a nightly CI job that compares headed vs. headless results — any divergence is flagged
  • Selector stability: Lint rule that warns when E2E tests use CSS class selectors instead of data-testid

Compare Test Data Factory Approaches: Builder Pattern vs. Fixture Files

1. Definition & Context

Test data factories generate realistic test data on demand rather than hardcoding values inline. Two common approaches are: Builder Pattern factories — programmatic functions that create objects with sensible defaults and allow selective overrides (e.g., buildContact({ name: 'Custom' })) — and Fixture files — static JSON/YAML files containing predefined test data loaded at test time. The Builder pattern offers flexibility and uniqueness per test; fixture files offer simplicity and readability but risk staleness and conflicts in parallel tests.

2. Real Project Use-Case — ContactsHub

In Manoj's ContactsHub, early tests used inline hardcoded data: { name: 'John', email: 'john@test.com' }. This worked until tests ran in parallel — two tests both created "john@test.com" and the unique email constraint caused random failures. Manoj switched to a Builder pattern factory: buildContact() generated unique emails using Date.now() and random suffixes. For the CSV import tests, he used fixture files (a static 500-contacts.csv) because the data format mattered more than uniqueness. The hybrid approach — factories for dynamic data, fixtures for static file formats — gave both flexibility and clarity.

3. Comparison Diagram

4. Code Example

// === BUILDER PATTERN FACTORY ===
// test/factories/contactFactory.ts
let counter = 0;

export function buildContact(overrides: Partial<Contact> = {}): Contact {
  counter++;
  const id = `${Date.now()}-${counter}`;
  return {
    name: `Test User ${counter}`,
    email: `user-${id}@test.com`,
    phone: `+91 ${String(9000000000 + counter)}`,
    avatar: null,
    tags: ['test'],
    createdAt: new Date(),
    ...overrides // selective override
  };
}

// Usage in tests:
it('creates a contact', async () => {
  const contact = buildContact({ name: 'Alice' });
  // contact.email is unique: "user-1709912345678-1@test.com"
  const res = await api.post('/api/contacts').send(contact);
  expect(res.status).toBe(201);
});

it('creates 10 contacts for list test', async () => {
  const contacts = Array.from({ length: 10 }, () => buildContact());
  // All 10 have unique emails — no conflicts
  await Promise.all(contacts.map(c => api.post('/api/contacts').send(c)));
});

// === FIXTURE FILE APPROACH ===
// test/fixtures/contacts.json
// [
//   { "name": "John Doe", "email": "john@test.com", "phone": "+91 9876543210" },
//   { "name": "Jane Smith", "email": "jane@test.com", "phone": "+91 9876543211" }
// ]

import contacts from '../fixtures/contacts.json';

it('renders contact list from fixture', () => {
  render(<ContactList contacts={contacts} />);
  expect(screen.getByText('John Doe')).toBeInTheDocument();
});

// === HYBRID: Factory + Fixture ===
// For CSV import tests — fixture file for the CSV format
import path from 'path';

it('imports contacts from CSV', async () => {
  const csvPath = path.join(__dirname, '../fixtures/500-contacts.csv');
  const res = await api.post('/api/contacts/import')
    .attach('file', csvPath);
  expect(res.body.imported).toBe(500);
});

5. Debugging Narrative

In ContactsHub, Manoj encountered a frustrating flaky test: displays contact list with pagination passed 9 out of 10 runs. The test used fixture data from contacts.json with hardcoded emails. When two Jest workers ran in parallel, both tried to insert "john@test.com" into MongoDB — one succeeded, one got a duplicate key error. The fix was migrating from fixture files to the Builder pattern factory for database-bound tests. Manoj kept fixture files only for non-DB scenarios (CSV upload format, JSON schema validation). After this change, the test suite ran at full parallelism (4 workers) with zero flakes for 200 consecutive CI runs.

6. Pitfall & Mitigation

PitfallMitigation
Factory generates data that doesn't match production schemas (missing required fields)Validate factory output against the same Zod/Joi schema used in production; update factory when schema changes
Fixture files become stale when DB schema evolvesAdd a CI step that validates fixture JSON against current schema; auto-generate fixtures from factory output
Builder pattern counter resets between test files, causing collisionsUse Date.now() + crypto.randomUUID() instead of a simple counter for guaranteed uniqueness

7. Validation Strategy

  • Parallel safety: Run test suite with --maxWorkers=8 ten consecutive times — zero failures indicates data isolation is working
  • Schema sync: Factory output is validated against the production Mongoose/Prisma schema in a dedicated test
  • Fixture freshness: CI step runs npm run validate-fixtures which parses all fixture files and reports schema mismatches
  • Coverage review: Ensure factories cover all required fields plus at least 3 edge cases (empty strings, special characters, maximum length)

Design a Test Data Factory System

1. Definition & Context

A test data factory system is a structured approach to generating realistic, isolated test data across your entire test suite. Rather than scattering hardcoded values across test files, factories centralize data creation with sensible defaults, support relational data (a leave request needs an employee, who needs a department), and guarantee uniqueness for parallel execution. A well-designed factory system reduces test setup boilerplate by 70% and eliminates data-related flakiness.

2. Real Project Use-Case — HR Leave Management System

In Manoj's HR Leave System, creating test data for the leave approval flow required 4 related entities: a department, a manager, an employee, and a leave request. Without factories, each test duplicated 20+ lines of setup code. Manoj built a factory system where buildLeaveRequest() automatically generated a valid employee, department, and manager with realistic data. Overriding was simple: buildLeaveRequest({ days: 15, type: 'sick' }) customized just what mattered while keeping defaults for everything else. This reduced test setup from 20 lines to 1 line per test.

3. Factory System Architecture

4. Code Example

// test/factories/index.ts — Complete factory system
import { faker } from '@faker-js/faker';

// Unique ID generator — safe for parallel tests
function uniqueId(): string {
  return `${Date.now()}-${Math.random().toString(36).slice(2, 8)}`;
}

// === Employee Factory ===
export function buildEmployee(overrides: Partial<Employee> = {}): Employee {
  const id = uniqueId();
  return {
    id: `emp-${id}`,
    name: faker.person.fullName(),
    email: `emp-${id}@test.com`,
    department: 'engineering',
    role: 'employee',
    leaveBalance: 20,
    managerId: null,
    createdAt: new Date(),
    ...overrides
  };
}

// === Leave Request Factory (auto-creates employee) ===
export function buildLeaveRequest(
  overrides: Partial<LeaveRequest> = {},
  employeeOverrides: Partial<Employee> = {}
): { leave: LeaveRequest; employee: Employee } {
  const employee = buildEmployee(employeeOverrides);
  const id = uniqueId();
  const leave: LeaveRequest = {
    id: `leave-${id}`,
    employeeId: employee.id,
    startDate: new Date('2025-06-02'),
    endDate: new Date('2025-06-06'),
    type: 'casual',
    status: 'pending',
    days: 5,
    reason: 'Family event',
    ...overrides
  };
  return { leave, employee };
}

// === Contact Factory (ContactsHub) ===
export function buildContact(overrides: Partial<Contact> = {}): Contact {
  const id = uniqueId();
  return {
    name: faker.person.fullName(),
    email: `contact-${id}@test.com`,
    phone: faker.phone.number('+91 ##########'),
    avatar: null,
    tags: ['test'],
    ...overrides
  };
}

// === Usage in tests ===
describe('LeaveService.approve', () => {
  it('deducts days from employee balance', async () => {
    // ONE LINE to create all needed test data
    const { leave, employee } = buildLeaveRequest({ days: 3 });

    await prisma.employee.create({ data: employee });
    await prisma.leave.create({ data: leave });

    await service.approve(leave.id);

    const updated = await prisma.employee.findUnique({
      where: { id: employee.id }
    });
    expect(updated.leaveBalance).toBe(17); // 20 - 3
  });

  it('rejects sick leave without medical certificate for 3+ days', async () => {
    const { leave, employee } = buildLeaveRequest({
      days: 5, type: 'sick' // only override what matters
    });
    await prisma.employee.create({ data: employee });
    await prisma.leave.create({ data: leave });

    const result = await service.approve(leave.id);
    expect(result.error).toContain('Medical certificate required');
  });
});

5. Debugging Narrative

In the HR Leave System, Manoj's factory initially generated dates using new Date() (current date). This caused a subtle bug: the test rejects backdated leave passed in the morning but failed after midnight — because the factory-generated date was "today" at creation time but became "yesterday" when the validation ran a few milliseconds later in a different timezone offset. Manoj fixed it by using explicit future dates in the factory defaults (startDate: new Date('2025-06-02')) instead of dynamic dates. He also added a factory validation test that verified all default dates are in the future relative to the test execution time.

6. Pitfall & Mitigation

PitfallMitigation
Factory defaults become unrealistic over time (e.g., dates in the past)Add a meta-test that validates all factory defaults against production constraints
Relational factories create too many database records, slowing testsUse buildEmployee() for in-memory-only tests; create a separate createEmployee() that persists to DB only when needed
Factory output doesn't match Prisma/Mongoose schema after migrationsType factories with the same TypeScript interfaces used by ORM — compiler catches mismatches immediately
Using faker makes test output non-deterministic and hard to debugSet faker.seed(12345) in test setup for reproducible data; use deterministic values for fields that assertions check

7. Validation Strategy

  • Schema compliance: Run factory output through the same Zod/Joi validation schema used in production routes
  • Uniqueness guarantee: Test that 1000 calls to buildContact() produce 1000 unique emails (no collisions)
  • Relational integrity: Verify buildLeaveRequest() produces a leave whose employeeId matches the returned employee's id
  • Performance: Factory instantiation must complete in under 1ms per call — benchmark in CI

Design Contract Tests for API Boundaries

1. Definition & Context

Contract testing verifies that the API producer (backend) and consumer (frontend or another service) agree on the request/response format — without requiring both to be running simultaneously. Unlike integration tests that test behavior, contract tests validate the shape of data exchanged: field names, types, required vs. optional fields, and status codes. If the backend changes a field from startDate to start_date, a contract test catches the mismatch before deployment, preventing runtime errors.

2. Real Project Use-Case — HR Leave Management System

In Manoj's HR Leave System, the Angular 18 frontend expected leave responses in the format { id: number, startDate: string, endDate: string, status: string }. A backend developer renamed startDate to start_date (following a new naming convention) without updating the frontend. The change passed all backend unit tests and integration tests — but broke the Angular leave calendar component. Manoj introduced contract tests using Zod schemas shared between frontend and backend. The schema defined the API contract, and both sides validated against it: the backend ensured its responses matched, and the frontend's HTTP service validated incoming data at runtime (in development mode).

3. Contract Testing Architecture

4. Code Example

// shared/contracts/leave.contract.ts — Shared between FE and BE
import { z } from 'zod';

export const LeaveResponseSchema = z.object({
  id: z.number(),
  employeeId: z.number(),
  startDate: z.string().datetime(),
  endDate: z.string().datetime(),
  type: z.enum(['casual', 'sick', 'earned']),
  status: z.enum(['pending', 'approved', 'rejected']),
  days: z.number().min(1),
  reason: z.string().optional()
});

export const LeaveListResponseSchema = z.object({
  data: z.array(LeaveResponseSchema),
  total: z.number(),
  page: z.number()
});

export type LeaveResponse = z.infer<typeof LeaveResponseSchema>;

// === PRODUCER TEST (Fastify backend) ===
// tests/contracts/leave.contract.test.ts
import { LeaveResponseSchema, LeaveListResponseSchema } from
  '@shared/contracts/leave.contract';
import { buildApp } from '../../app';

describe('Leave API Contract', () => {
  let app;
  beforeAll(async () => { app = await buildApp(); });
  afterAll(async () => { await app.close(); });

  it('GET /api/leaves returns data matching LeaveListResponseSchema', async () => {
    const res = await app.inject({ method: 'GET', url: '/api/leaves',
      headers: { authorization: `Bearer ${testToken}` }
    });
    const body = JSON.parse(res.body);
    // Contract validation — throws if shape doesn't match
    const result = LeaveListResponseSchema.safeParse(body);
    expect(result.success).toBe(true);
    if (!result.success) console.error(result.error.issues);
  });

  it('POST /api/leaves returns data matching LeaveResponseSchema', async () => {
    const res = await app.inject({
      method: 'POST', url: '/api/leaves',
      headers: { authorization: `Bearer ${testToken}` },
      payload: { startDate: '2025-06-02', endDate: '2025-06-06', type: 'casual' }
    });
    const result = LeaveResponseSchema.safeParse(JSON.parse(res.body));
    expect(result.success).toBe(true);
  });
});

// === CONSUMER TEST (Angular frontend) ===
// leave.service.spec.ts
import { LeaveListResponseSchema } from '@shared/contracts/leave.contract';

it('HTTP mock matches the shared contract schema', () => {
  // The mock data used in component tests must also be valid
  const mockResponse = {
    data: [{ id: 1, employeeId: 42, startDate: '2025-06-02T00:00:00.000Z',
      endDate: '2025-06-06T00:00:00.000Z', type: 'casual',
      status: 'pending', days: 5 }],
    total: 1, page: 1
  };
  const result = LeaveListResponseSchema.safeParse(mockResponse);
  expect(result.success).toBe(true);
});

5. Debugging Narrative

After the startDate → start_date incident, Manoj set up the shared Zod schema package. A week later, a backend developer added a new field approvedBy: number | null to the leave response but forgot to update the shared schema. The frontend didn't break immediately (it ignored the extra field), but when the frontend later tried to display approvedBy, it used the old type assumption (string instead of number). The contract test caught this during the next schema sync: the producer test failed because the response included approvedBy which wasn't in the schema. This prompted the developer to update the shared schema, which TypeScript then propagated to the frontend code.

6. Pitfall & Mitigation

PitfallMitigation
Shared schema package becomes stale — nobody updates itCI step: run contract tests on every backend PR; fail if response doesn't match schema
Frontend mock data passes contract test but real API returns different dataRun producer contract tests against the actual API endpoint, not just mock data
Schema is too strict — breaks on optional fields or new additionsUse .passthrough() in Zod for forward compatibility — extra fields are allowed but validated fields must match

7. Validation Strategy

  • Bi-directional testing: Both producer and consumer CI pipelines validate against the same schema — a schema change requires both sides to update
  • Schema versioning: Use npm package versioning for the shared schema; pin versions in frontend and backend package.json
  • Breaking change detection: CI compares current schema with previous version; flag removals or type changes as breaking
  • Runtime validation (dev): In development mode, Angular HTTP interceptor validates every API response against the contract schema and logs warnings

Debug Contract Test Failures

1. Definition & Context

Debugging contract test failures requires understanding which side broke the contract — did the producer (backend) change its response format, or did the consumer (frontend) update its expectations? Contract tests fail at the schema level, so the error messages show field mismatches, type errors, or missing required fields. The debugging process involves comparing the expected schema with the actual API response, identifying which deployment introduced the change, and deciding whether to update the schema or revert the code change.

2. Real Project Use-Case — CRM Sales Dashboard

In Manoj's CRM Sales Dashboard, a contract test for the deals endpoint failed with: Expected number for 'value', received string. The Express.js backend was returning value: "50000" (string from MongoDB) instead of value: 50000 (number). This happened because a new developer added a MongoDB aggregation pipeline that returned string values for numeric fields, and the Express route handler didn't cast them back to numbers. The Angular Recharts component silently rendered wrong bar heights (treating "50000" as NaN), creating a visual bug nobody noticed until the contract test caught it.

3. Debug Flow Diagram

4. Debugging Code Example

// CRM Dashboard — Contract test failure debugging
import { z } from 'zod';

// Shared contract schema
const DealResponseSchema = z.object({
  id: z.string(),
  title: z.string(),
  value: z.number(),  // Expected: number
  stage: z.enum(['lead', 'proposal', 'negotiation', 'closed_won', 'closed_lost']),
  owner: z.string()
});

// Producer contract test that caught the bug
describe('Deal API Contract', () => {
  it('GET /api/deals/:id matches DealResponseSchema', async () => {
    const res = await request(app).get('/api/deals/deal-1')
      .set('Authorization', `Bearer ${token}`);

    const result = DealResponseSchema.safeParse(res.body);

    // FAILED with:
    // {
    //   code: 'invalid_type',
    //   expected: 'number',
    //   received: 'string',
    //   path: ['value'],
    //   message: 'Expected number, received string'
    // }

    if (!result.success) {
      // Debug: log the actual response to see what changed
      console.log('Actual response:', JSON.stringify(res.body, null, 2));
      console.log('Schema errors:', JSON.stringify(result.error.issues, null, 2));
    }
    expect(result.success).toBe(true);
  });
});

// ROOT CAUSE: MongoDB aggregation returns strings
// Express route — BEFORE (bug)
router.get('/api/deals/:id', async (req, res) => {
  const deal = await Deal.aggregate([
    { $match: { _id: req.params.id } },
    { $project: { title: 1, value: { $toString: '$value' }, stage: 1, owner: 1 } }
    //                                ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^ BUG: $toString
  ]);
  res.json(deal[0]);
});

// Express route — AFTER (fix: remove $toString, add Number cast)
router.get('/api/deals/:id', async (req, res) => {
  const deal = await Deal.findById(req.params.id)
    .select('title value stage owner')
    .lean();
  res.json({
    ...deal,
    value: Number(deal.value) // ensure numeric type
  });
});

5. Debugging Narrative

Manoj's debugging process for the CRM deal value contract failure:

  1. CI error: Contract test reported Expected number for 'value', received string on the GET /api/deals/:id endpoint.
  2. Verify locally: Used curl http://localhost:3000/api/deals/deal-1 — confirmed value: "50000" (string, not number).
  3. Git blame: git log --oneline -10 -- routes/deals.ts showed a commit from yesterday: "optimize deals query with aggregation pipeline." The new aggregation used $toString on the value field (copy-paste from a reporting query that needed string formatting).
  4. Impact analysis: The Recharts bar chart used deal.value for bar height — JavaScript's implicit coercion made "50000" work in some operations but fail in Math.max() comparisons, causing incorrect chart scaling.
  5. Fix: Removed $toString from the aggregation, added explicit Number() cast in the route handler, and added a contract test for the deals list endpoint too.

6. Pitfall & Mitigation

PitfallMitigation
MongoDB aggregations silently change field types (number → string)Add contract tests for every endpoint that uses aggregation pipelines; validate output types explicitly
Contract test passes in isolation but fails against real seeded dataContract tests should run against the same database state as integration tests; use shared seed data
Fixing the backend breaks the frontend's expectationsAlways update the shared schema first, then update both producer and consumer in the same PR or coordinated PRs

7. Validation Strategy

  • Type strictness: Contract schemas should use z.number() not z.coerce.number() — coercion hides type bugs
  • Regression guard: After fixing a contract failure, add the specific failing field as a dedicated test case with a comment explaining the incident
  • Cross-repo sync: If frontend and backend are in separate repos, use a shared npm package for contract schemas; CI in both repos runs contract tests
  • API response logging: In development, add middleware that validates every response against the contract schema and logs warnings for mismatches
